name: Energy Level Diagrams
summary: Visual representations of the quantised energy states of a system — used in atomic physics, nuclear physics, molecular physics, and solid-state physics.
whenToUseIt: Use energy level diagrams whenever you need to understand transitions between states, spectral lines, band gaps in semiconductors, or nuclear decay pathways.
steps: Identify the system (atom, nucleus, molecule, solid)
Calculate or look up the allowed energy levels
Draw horizontal lines at each energy level, with spacing proportional to energy differences
Mark transitions (arrows) between levels, labelling with the energy/wavelength of emitted or absorbed photons
Identify selection rules that determine which transitions are allowed
example: The hydrogen atom has energy levels E_n = -13.6/n² eV. The transition from n=3 to n=2 emits a photon with energy 1.89 eV (wavelength 656 nm — visible red light). This is the H-alpha line, visible in the emission spectra of nebulae.
